walkthrough.title: Lay Out the Race Track
walkthrough.text: Welcome to Cubitos! This is a dice-building race game where you will roll dice, push your luck, and sprint around a track. First, grab the race track board and lay it in the center of the table. The track wraps around the board with numbered spaces. Some spaces have bonus icons on them — we will talk about those later. For now, just make sure everyone can see the whole track.

walkthrough.title: Choose Ability Cards
walkthrough.text: Cubitos has eight different die colors, and each color has multiple ability cards that change what that color does. Before the game, your group picks one ability card per color. You can choose randomly, pick a recommended set from the rulebook, or draft them. Lay the eight chosen ability cards along the edge of the board so everyone can read them. These cards define the market for this game, and they make every session feel different.

walkthrough.title: Fill the Market
walkthrough.text: Now stock the dice market. Each ability card has a supply of matching colored dice placed next to it. The exact number depends on the player count — check the card or rulebook for the right amount. These are the dice players will buy during the game to upgrade their pools. Make sure each color's supply is clearly separated so players can easily see what is available and how many are left.

walkthrough.title: Prepare Starting Dice
walkthrough.text: Each player takes nine starting gray dice. These are your basic dice. They are not exciting — each gray die has mostly blank faces and only one or two hit faces. But they are yours, and they are all going into your draw bag. Grab a cloth bag and drop all nine gray dice inside. Give the bag a good shake.

walkthrough.title: Set Up Player Areas
walkthrough.text: Each player takes a player board or area in front of them. You will need space for three zones: your Active zone where you place dice showing hits, your rolled zone for dice you are currently rolling, and your discard zone for spent dice. Also grab a player marker and place it on the starting space of the race track. Everyone lines up at the start.

walkthrough.title: Draw Your First Hand
walkthrough.text: Reach into your bag and draw nine dice — that is all of them for the first round. Place them in your rolling area. You are ready to go. The player who most recently ran somewhere in real life goes first, or just pick someone. Play will proceed in a specific order based on track position, but for the very first Roll Phase, everyone rolls simultaneously. Let's learn how to play!

bullets: Place the race track board centrally
Track has numbered spaces, some with bonus icons

bullets: 8 die colors, each with multiple ability card options
Pick 1 ability card per color (random, recommended, or drafted)
Lay cards along the board — these define the market for this game

summary.title: Fill the Market
bullets: Stock colored dice next to each ability card
Supply count varies by player count
Keep each color clearly separated

summary.title: Prepare Starting Dice
bullets: Each player takes 9 gray starting dice
Gray dice have mostly blanks, 1-2 hit faces
Place all 9 in your cloth draw bag, shake well

summary.title: Set Up Player Areas
bullets: Set up Active zone (hits), Rolling zone, and Discard zone
Take a player marker, place on starting space of the track

summary.title: Draw Your First Hand
bullets: Draw all 9 dice from your bag
Place them in your rolling area
Choose a first player — Roll Phase is simultaneous

walkthrough.title: Game Overview
walkthrough.text: Cubitos is a race. The first player to cross the finish line wins. There is no point scoring, no victory point salad — just get around the track faster than everyone else. Each round has two phases: the Roll Phase, where everyone simultaneously rolls dice and pushes their luck, and the Run Phase, where players take turns spending their hits to move, buy dice, and use abilities. Then you clean up and do it again.

walkthrough.title: The Roll Phase: First Roll
walkthrough.text: Draw dice from your bag until you have at least nine dice in your hand. If your bag is empty before you hit nine, shuffle all your discarded dice back into the bag and keep drawing. Now roll all of your dice at once. Each die face shows either a colored symbol, which is called a hit, or a blank white face. Any die showing a hit gets moved to your Active zone. Any die showing blank stays in your rolling area.

walkthrough.title: The Roll Phase: Re-Rolling
walkthrough.text: Here is where it gets exciting. After your first roll, you look at your blank dice and decide: do you want to re-roll them? You can pick up all your blank dice and roll them again, hoping for more hits. Any new hits move to your Active zone. Any new blanks stay in the rolling area. You can keep re-rolling your blanks as many times as you want. Each successful re-roll adds more hits to your Active zone, making your turn more powerful.

walkthrough.title: Busting
walkthrough.text: But here is the catch — push your luck too far and you bust. If you re-roll your blank dice and every single one comes up blank again, that is a bust. All of your hits in the Active zone are lost. Every die you rolled this phase goes to your discard pile. As a consolation, you do get to move one space forward on the track. Busting hurts, but it is not the end of the world. The one-space consolation move keeps you in the race.

walkthrough.title: Stopping Safely
walkthrough.text: You do not have to keep re-rolling. At any point during the Roll Phase, after any re-roll, you can choose to stop. When you stop, all the hits in your Active zone are locked in and ready to spend during the Run Phase. The remaining blanks in your rolling area go straight to your discard pile. Knowing when to stop is the key skill in Cubitos. Two solid hits might be enough. Pushing for a third could bust you and lose everything.

walkthrough.title: The Run Phase: Turn Order
walkthrough.text: Once every player has either stopped or busted, the Run Phase begins. Unlike the Roll Phase, the Run Phase is not simultaneous. Players take turns in track order — the player furthest back on the track goes first. If players are tied on the same space, the one who arrived there most recently goes first. This catch-up mechanism is important because going first in the Run Phase lets you buy from the market before others.

walkthrough.title: The Run Phase: Spending Hits
walkthrough.text: Different colored hits do different things based on the ability cards chosen during setup. But the two most common resources are fans and money. Fan symbols let you move forward on the race track — typically one space per fan. Money symbols let you buy new dice from the market. Some colored dice grant special abilities like extra movement, bonus draws, or the ability to skip spaces. Check the ability cards to see what each color does.

walkthrough.title: Buying New Dice
walkthrough.text: When you spend money hits, you can buy one or more dice from the market. Each die has a cost printed on its ability card. You pay the money, take the die from the supply, and place it in your discard pile — not your bag. It will get shuffled into your bag later when your bag runs out. This is exactly like deck-building games: new purchases go to discard, and when your draw bag is empty, you shuffle your entire discard pile into a fresh bag. Better dice mean better rolls in future rounds.

walkthrough.title: Moving on the Track
walkthrough.text: When you spend fan hits, you move your marker forward on the race track. Each fan typically moves you one space, though some abilities grant bonus movement. As you land on or pass through spaces, check for track bonuses. Some spaces give you a free die from the market, extra fans, money, or other rewards. These bonuses trigger as you move through them, so a big movement turn can chain multiple bonuses together.

walkthrough.title: Cleanup and Next Round
walkthrough.text: After every player has completed their Run Phase turn, the round ends. Move all dice from your Active zone to your discard pile. Then draw a new hand of dice from your bag — at least nine dice. If your bag is empty or runs short, shuffle your entire discard pile back into the bag and keep drawing. Then a new Roll Phase begins. The game continues round after round until someone crosses the finish line.

walkthrough.title: Winning the Game
walkthrough.text: The first player to land on or pass the finish line space wins the game immediately. There is no final round and no tiebreaker scoring. It is a pure race. If you cross the finish line during your Run Phase movement, the game ends right then and there. Every decision you make — which dice to buy, when to stop rolling, how to spend your hits — is all in service of getting around that track first.

summary.title: Game Overview
bullets: First player to cross the finish line wins — pure race, no VP scoring
Each round: Roll Phase (simultaneous) then Run Phase (track order)
Repeat until someone crosses the finish line

walkthrough.title: Welcome & Meet the Players
walkthrough.text: In this practice tutorial, we will walk through a scripted opening with four example players — Player 1 through Player 4. Each player will make different choices so you can see how the game flows. Player 4 will make some common mistakes so you can learn from them. Follow along even if you have fewer players at your table. Let's roll!

walkthrough.title: Starting Position
walkthrough.text: All four players have their markers on the starting space. Everyone has nine gray dice in their bag. The market is stocked with colored dice, and the eight ability cards are laid out for everyone to see. Each player draws all nine dice from their bag. Time for the first Roll Phase — remember, everyone rolls simultaneously.

walkthrough.title: First Roll Phase: Everyone Rolls
walkthrough.text: All four players roll their nine gray dice at the same time. Gray dice are not great — they have mostly blank faces. Player 1 gets three hits and six blanks. Player 2 gets two hits and seven blanks. Player 3 gets four hits and five blanks — nice roll! Player 4 gets one hit and eight blanks. Hits go to the Active zone, blanks stay in the rolling area.

walkthrough.title: Re-Roll Decisions
walkthrough.text: Now everyone decides whether to push their luck and re-roll their blanks. Player 1 has three hits already, which is decent. They decide to play it safe and stop. Player 2 has two hits — not great but workable. They re-roll their seven blanks and get two more hits. Now they have four hits total and five blanks. Player 2 decides to stop there. Player 3 already has four hits, a great roll. They stop immediately — no need to risk it.

walkthrough.title: Player 4's Mistake: Pushing Too Hard
walkthrough.text: Player 4 has only one hit and eight blanks. That is rough, so they decide to re-roll. They roll all eight blanks and get three hits. Now they have four hits and five blanks — that is actually a solid position! But Player 4 gets greedy. They want even more hits, so they re-roll the five blanks. Every single one comes up blank again. That is a bust. Player 4 loses all four hits in their Active zone. Every die goes to discard. As a consolation, Player 4 moves one space forward on the track.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Know When to Stop
walkthrough.text: Player 4 had four hits — a perfectly good result — but got greedy and lost everything. The bust rule is brutal: if ALL your re-rolled dice come up blank, you lose every hit you accumulated. The more blanks you re-roll, the lower the chance they all come up blank, but with gray dice, the odds of busting are real. With four or more hits in your Active zone, stopping is almost always the right call unless you desperately need a huge turn.

walkthrough.title: Run Phase Begins
walkthrough.text: The Roll Phase is over. Now the Run Phase begins. Since all players are near the start, the order goes by most recent arrival. Player 4 goes first since they already moved one consolation space. Then Players 1, 2, and 3 take their turns in track order. Remember, the player furthest back goes first in the Run Phase, which is a nice catch-up mechanism.

walkthrough.title: Player 1's Run Phase: Spending Hits
walkthrough.text: Player 1 has three gray hits. Gray hits on the starter dice typically give you one fan or one money each, depending on the face. Let's say Player 1 got two fan hits and one money hit. They spend the two fans to move two spaces forward on the track. Then they spend the one money to check the market — but most colored dice cost two or three money. Player 1 cannot afford anything yet, so they save nothing and discard the dice. Moving two spaces is a solid first turn.

walkthrough.title: Player 2's Run Phase: First Purchase
walkthrough.text: Player 2 has four hits — let's say two fans and two money. They move two spaces forward with their fans. With two money, they can afford a cheap colored die from the market. Player 2 buys a green die and places it in their discard pile. That green die will show up in their bag next time they reshuffle. It has better faces than gray dice — more hits and a special ability. This is dice-building in action.

walkthrough.title: Player 3's Run Phase: Strong Start
walkthrough.text: Player 3 rolled four hits — three fans and one money. They move three spaces forward, passing through a track bonus space that gives them a free extra fan of movement. So Player 3 actually moves four spaces total. Nice — track bonuses can really add up. They spend their one money but cannot afford a die either. Still, four spaces of movement on the first turn puts Player 3 in the lead.

walkthrough.title: Player 4's Run Phase: The Bust Recovery
walkthrough.text: Player 4 busted, so they already took their one consolation space of movement. They have no hits to spend, so their Run Phase is done immediately. While everyone else is racing ahead, Player 4 is stuck. This is why busting is painful — you lose an entire round of meaningful action. But the catch-up mechanism means Player 4 will go first in the next Run Phase, so they will get first pick of the market.

walkthrough.title: Round 2: Draw and Roll Again
walkthrough.text: All dice move to the discard pile after the Run Phase. Everyone draws nine dice from their bag. Since your bag had exactly nine to start and they are now in the discard pile, you shuffle your discard pile back into the bag and draw all nine again. Player 2 is excited because their new green die is now shuffled in! This time, a new simultaneous Roll Phase begins.

walkthrough.title: Player 4's Mistake: Buying Without Focus
walkthrough.text: In round two, Player 4 rolls decently and does not bust. They have three money to spend. Instead of buying one strong die, Player 4 buys three cheap dice of three different colors. More dice sounds good, right? But here is the problem: Player 4 now has twelve dice total, and only three of them are colored. That means most rolls will still be full of weak gray blanks. Buying lots of cheap dice dilutes your pool — you are less likely to draw the good ones.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Quality Over Quantity
walkthrough.text: When buying dice, focus on quality over quantity. One expensive die with great faces will improve your average roll more than three cheap dice with mediocre faces. Also, think about synergy — some colors work together. Check the ability cards and plan which two or three colors complement each other. A focused strategy with a few strong dice beats a scattered collection of weak ones.

walkthrough.title: Player 4's Mistake: Ignoring Track Bonuses
walkthrough.text: A few rounds in, Player 4 has a choice: move four spaces to an empty stretch of track, or move three spaces to land on a bonus space that gives a free die. Player 4 picks the four-space move because more movement equals faster, right? Wrong — that free die from the bonus space is worth more than one extra space of movement. Track bonuses are powerful and should factor into your movement decisions every single turn.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Plan Your Movement Around Bonuses
walkthrough.text: Always check what track bonus spaces are coming up before spending your fans. Sometimes moving one fewer space to land on a bonus is far better than moving as far as possible. Free dice, extra fans, and bonus money from track spaces compound over the game. Players who plan their landing spots around bonuses consistently outperform players who just race blindly forward.

walkthrough.title: Continue Playing
walkthrough.text: The game continues round after round. Each round, you draw dice, roll simultaneously, push your luck or stop, then take turns spending hits in track order. As you buy more colored dice, your rolls get better and your turns get more powerful. You will start seeing big combos — a fan die plus a movement ability die chaining together for huge advances on the track. Keep buying smart, keep landing on bonuses, and keep pushing your luck wisely.

walkthrough.title: Congratulations
walkthrough.text: You have seen the Roll Phase, the bust rule, the Run Phase, and how buying dice works. You watched Player 4 learn from three common mistakes: pushing too hard and busting, buying too many weak dice without focus, and ignoring track bonuses. Remember: stop while you are ahead, buy quality dice that synergize, and plan your movement around bonus spaces. Now race for real!

walkthrough.title: Common Mistakes to Avoid
walkthrough.text: Before we get into strategy, here are the mistakes that trip up new Cubitos players the most. First, pushing re-rolls when you already have a decent result — greed is the number one cause of busting. Second, buying lots of cheap dice instead of saving for one great die — quantity dilutes your bag and makes your draws worse. Third, ignoring track bonus spaces — those free dice and extra movement are game-changers. Fourth, forgetting that the Run Phase goes in reverse track order — being behind has a built-in advantage. Keep these in mind and you will avoid the biggest beginner traps.

walkthrough.title: When to Push and When to Stop
walkthrough.text: The push-your-luck decision is the heartbeat of Cubitos. Here is a practical framework. If you have zero or one hit after your first roll, you should almost always re-roll — you have nothing to lose. If you have two or three hits, consider re-rolling once more, especially if you still have many blanks. The more blanks you re-roll, the less likely they ALL come up blank, so a large re-roll is actually safer than a small one. If you have four or more hits, stop unless you desperately need a huge turn to catch up. The risk of losing four solid hits is almost never worth the marginal gain of one or two more.

walkthrough.title: Dice-Building Strategy
walkthrough.text: Think of your dice bag like a deck in a deck-building game. Every die you add changes your average draw quality. Early in the game, focus on dice that give you money or fans reliably — you need both to move and to keep upgrading. As the game progresses, shift toward dice that provide burst movement or powerful abilities. Do not buy more than ten to twelve dice total. A lean bag with strong dice is far better than a bloated bag where you rarely draw your good ones.

walkthrough.title: Reading the Ability Cards
walkthrough.text: Before your first roll, study the eight ability cards on display. These define the entire game. Some cards turn a die color into a movement powerhouse. Others give money engines or defensive abilities. Look for synergies between two or three colors. For example, if one color gives you fans and another gives you bonus fans when you already have fans, those two colors work beautifully together. Plan your buying strategy before the first round, not during it.

walkthrough.title: Track Awareness
walkthrough.text: Always know what bonus spaces are coming up on the track. Before spending your fans, count the spaces ahead and check where the bonuses are. Sometimes moving one fewer space to land on a bonus is worth far more than pushing ahead to an empty space. Free dice from the track are essentially free money — you get a colored die without spending any hits. Those bonus dice can snowball your bag quality. Some spaces also grant extra fans or money, which can extend your turn dramatically.

walkthrough.title: Run Phase Turn Order Advantage
walkthrough.text: Remember that the player furthest back on the track goes first in the Run Phase. This is a powerful catch-up mechanism. Going first means you get first pick of the market — the best dice are available before anyone else buys them. It also means you resolve your movement before leaders, so you can plan your landing more precisely. Do not panic if you are behind. The turn order advantage, combined with smart dice buying, can close the gap quickly.

walkthrough.title: Balancing Fans and Money
walkthrough.text: New players often focus too much on one resource. If you only buy movement dice, you will run fast but never improve your bag. If you only buy money dice, you will have an amazing collection but never advance on the track. The sweet spot is roughly sixty percent fans and forty percent money in the early game, gradually shifting to mostly fans in the late game. Once you have three or four strong colored dice, stop buying and start sprinting. The finish line does not care how fancy your dice are.

walkthrough.title: Late Game Racing
walkthrough.text: In the final stretch of the game, your priorities shift. Stop buying dice entirely — there is no time for new purchases to cycle into your bag. Spend everything on fans and movement. Push your luck a little harder than usual because the payoff of a big movement turn can end the game on the spot. But do not bust — losing a turn this close to the finish is devastating. If you are in the lead, play conservatively. If you are behind, take calculated risks. The finish line is all that matters.

summary.title: Common Mistakes to Avoid
bullets: Don't push re-rolls when you already have 4+ hits — greed causes busts
Don't buy lots of cheap dice — quantity dilutes your bag
Don't ignore track bonus spaces — free dice and movement are huge
Remember Run Phase goes in reverse track order — being behind has perks

More blanks re-rolled = less likely to bust (larger pool is safer)

Moving 1 fewer space to land on a bonus is often worth it

Spend everything on fans and movement
If leading, play conservatively; if behind, take calculated risks
Busting near the finish is devastating — don't throw it away

walkthrough.title: Opening Dice Evaluation
walkthrough.text: The first two rounds set the tone for the entire game. Before rolling a single die, study the eight ability cards and identify which colored dice offer the best value. Look for dice that have high hit-to-blank ratios — more hits per face means fewer busts and more consistent turns. Then look at what those hits do. A die that gives two fans per hit is twice as good as one that gives one fan. Prioritize dice that give you the most movement per money spent. Your first purchase should be a die that immediately improves your rolling consistency.

walkthrough.title: The Sprint Strategy
walkthrough.text: This focuses on maximum movement as fast as possible. Buy dice that provide fans and movement bonuses, ignore everything else. The idea is to get so far ahead on the track that you collect track bonuses before anyone else can reach them, and you cross the finish line before opponents build their engines. This works best on shorter tracks or when the fan dice available are particularly strong. The risk is that if opponents build better dice pools, they will catch up with bigger turns in the late game.

walkthrough.title: The Engine Strategy
walkthrough.text: Prioritizes money and dice-buying in the early game, accepting slower movement initially. Buy two or three high-quality dice in rounds one through three, then shift entirely to movement. By mid-game, your dice pool is so strong that every roll generates massive hits, and your turns produce far more fans than sprint players can manage. This strategy requires patience and relies on the catch-up mechanism — staying behind to go first in the Run Phase and getting first pick of the market.

walkthrough.title: Bag Management
walkthrough.text: Advanced Cubitos is all about bag management. Track which dice are in your bag versus your discard pile. If you just bought a great die, it is in your discard — you won't draw it until your bag empties and you reshuffle. This means timing your purchases matters. Buy when your bag is about to run out so new dice enter your draws sooner. Also consider bag size — a bag of ten dice with three strong colored ones means you draw them almost every round. A bag of fifteen with three strong ones means you might go multiple rounds without seeing them.

walkthrough.title: Color Synergy Combos
walkthrough.text: The variable ability cards create different combos every game, so you need to identify synergies on the fly. Look for combos like a color that gives bonus fans when you have money hits active, paired with a color that reliably gives money. Or a color that lets you draw extra dice, combined with a color that has high hit rates to capitalize on those extra draws. The best players spot these combos during setup and build their entire strategy around two or three colors that amplify each other.

walkthrough.title: Track Bonus Route Planning
walkthrough.text: Expert players plan multiple turns ahead on the track. Look at the bonus spaces three to five spaces ahead and figure out how many fans you need to land on the best ones. Some bonus spaces give free dice worth three or four money — that is an entire turn's worth of purchasing for free. When you see a cluster of bonus spaces ahead, time your big movement turns to chain through them. Conversely, if the next few spaces are empty, consider spending money this turn and saving movement for when bonus spaces appear.

walkthrough.title: Push-Your-Luck Mathematics
walkthrough.text: Understanding the math behind busting helps you make better decisions. A bust only happens when ALL re-rolled dice come up blank. If you are re-rolling five gray dice and each gray die has roughly a one-in-three chance of hitting, the probability of all five being blank is approximately thirteen percent. With four gray dice, the bust chance rises to around twenty percent. With three, about thirty percent. The key insight: re-rolling more dice is actually safer than re-rolling fewer dice, because it is harder for all of them to miss. But once you are down to two or three blanks, the bust risk climbs sharply.

walkthrough.title: Adapting to Opponents
walkthrough.text: Cubitos is a race, and you need to watch your opponents. If someone is sprinting ahead on the track, you cannot afford to spend three rounds building an engine — you need to match their pace or risk losing. If everyone is buying slowly, you can afford to invest in your bag because the finish line is far away. Watch which dice your opponents buy and how many they have. If someone has a lean bag of killer dice, expect them to make big moves soon. If someone busted twice in a row, they are likely to play conservatively now. Adapt your risk tolerance and spending to the state of the race, not just your own board.

summary.title: Opening Dice Evaluation
bullets: Study ability cards before the first roll — identify best value dice
Look for high hit-to-blank ratios for consistency
Prioritize fans per money spent
First purchase should immediately improve rolling consistency

summary.title: The Sprint Strategy
bullets: Focus entirely on movement dice — race ahead fast
Collect track bonuses before opponents reach them
Works best on short tracks or with strong fan dice
Risk: opponents with better pools catch up in late game

summary.title: The Engine Strategy
bullets: Prioritize money and buying in rounds 1-3, accept slower movement
Build 2-3 high-quality dice, then shift entirely to movement
Mid-game rolls generate massive hits for huge fan turns
Rely on catch-up mechanism — stay behind for first market picks

summary.title: Bag Management
bullets: Track which dice are in bag vs. discard pile
Buy when bag is nearly empty so new dice enter draws sooner
Lean bag (10 dice) draws strong dice almost every round
Bloated bag (15+) may go rounds without seeing key dice

summary.title: Color Synergy Combos
bullets: Variable ability cards create different combos every game
Look for colors that amplify each other (bonus fans + money, etc.)
Spot combos during setup and build your strategy around them
Focus on 2-3 synergistic colors, not all 8

summary.title: Track Bonus Route Planning
bullets: Plan 3-5 spaces ahead on the track
Time big movement turns to chain through bonus space clusters
Free dice from bonuses = an entire turn of purchasing for free
If next spaces are empty, spend money now, save movement for later

summary.title: Push-Your-Luck Mathematics
bullets: Bust = ALL re-rolled dice blank (not just some)
5 gray dice re-rolled: ~13% bust chance
3 gray dice re-rolled: ~30% bust chance
More dice = safer re-roll; fewer dice = sharply rising bust risk

summary.title: Adapting to Opponents
bullets: If someone sprints ahead, match their pace — no time for slow building
If everyone buys slowly, invest in your bag quality
Watch opponents' dice pools — lean killer bags mean big moves coming
Adapt risk tolerance to the state of the race, not just your own board

entries.definition: A die face showing a colored symbol. Hits are placed in your Active zone and can be spent during the Run Phase for movement, money, or abilities. The opposite of a blank.
A die face showing no colored symbol — just a white or empty face. Blanks stay in your rolling area and can be re-rolled. If all re-rolled dice come up blank, you bust.

When you re-roll your blank dice and every single one comes up blank again. You lose all hits in your Active zone. All dice go to your discard pile. As consolation, you move one space forward on the track.
When you bust, you automatically move one space forward on the race track. This prevents a bust from being a total loss and keeps busted players in the race.

The cloth bag holding your dice pool. You draw dice from it each round. When the bag is empty, shuffle all dice from your discard pile back into the bag and continue drawing. Similar to a draw deck in deck-building games.
The area where you place dice showing hits during the Roll Phase. These hits are available to spend during the Run Phase. If you bust, all dice in the Active zone are lost to the discard pile.
The first phase of each round. All players roll simultaneously. Roll your dice, set aside hits, then decide whether to re-roll blanks or stop. Continues until every player has stopped or busted.
The second phase of each round. Players take turns in track order (furthest back goes first). Spend hits for fans (movement), money (buy dice), and special abilities based on die colors.
A resource gained from certain die hits. Fans are spent to move your marker forward on the race track, typically one space per fan. Some abilities grant bonus fan movement.
A resource gained from certain die hits. Money is spent to buy new colored dice from the market. Each die has a cost printed on its ability card.

The supply of colored dice available for purchase, arranged by color next to their ability cards. Dice are bought during the Run Phase using money hits. Supply is limited and shared among all players.

Eight cards (one per die color) that define what each colored die does in the current game. Chosen during setup from multiple options per color, making every game different. Show the die's cost, hit effects, and special abilities.
The nine basic starting dice every player begins with. Gray dice have mostly blank faces and only one or two hit faces, making them weak but necessary. They provide small amounts of fans or money.

Cubitos features eight different die colors beyond gray, each with a unique ability defined by the chosen ability card. Colors include options that provide fans, money, special movement, extra draws, and other abilities. The exact powers change each game based on which ability cards are selected.

Special rewards on certain spaces of the race track. When you land on or pass through a bonus space, you receive the indicated reward — such as a free die from the market, extra fans, bonus money, or other benefits. Planning movement around bonus spaces is a key strategy.

During the Run Phase, the player furthest back on the track goes first. If tied, the player who arrived at that space most recently goes first. This catch-up mechanism gives trailing players first pick of the market.

Where spent dice and busted dice go after each round. When your draw bag is empty, shuffle your entire discard pile back into the bag. Newly purchased dice also go to the discard pile first, entering your bag on the next reshuffle.
The final space on the race track. The first player to land on or pass the finish line wins immediately. There is no final round or additional scoring — Cubitos is a pure race.

When your draw bag is empty and you need more dice, take all dice from your discard pile, place them in the bag, and shake well. This is how newly purchased dice enter your draws — they cycle from discard into the bag on the next reshuffle.
